question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run a victim's pending setIsAuthorized transaction in the mempool under the precondition that the victim has broadcast a signed Authorization with nonce==nonce[victim] to authorize an agent for take/repay/withdraw, by submitting the identical (authorization, signature) tuple first, consuming nonce[victim] and incrementing it, so the victim's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce, blocking the agent authorization indefinitely at the cost of a single transaction? Invariant tested: a valid signed authorization must be processable by its intended submitter without front-run invalidation. Scoped impact: DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claimFee via blocked agent authorization. Proof idea: deploy EcrecoverAuthorizer, have victim sign Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=agent, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=far), attacker submits same tuple first, assert victim's submission reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.isAuthorized] Can an already-authorized agent (signer satisfying IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer)==true) call setIsAuthorized with a self-signed Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=currentNonce) to increment nonce[victim] at will, under the precondition that the attacker was previously authorized by victim on Midnight, thereby invalidating any pending signed authorizations victim has distributed to legitimate agents? Invariant tested: nonce[authorizer] must only be incrementable by the authorizer themselves or with their explicit consent. Scoped impact: DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, claimFee by invalidating all pending signed authorizations. Proof idea: authorize attacker on Midnight, attacker calls setIsAuthorized with self-signed auth for victim, assert nonce[victim] incremented and victim's pre-signed auth now reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=EcrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to revoke the EcrecoverAuthorizer's own authorization on Midnight, under the precondition that the attacker is authorized by victim on Midnight and EcrecoverAuthorizer is currently authorized on Midnight by victim, permanently blocking victim from using EcrecoverAuthorizer to authorize future agents needed for take/repay/withdraw/liquidate? Invariant tested: an authorized agent must not be able to revoke the authorization infrastructure itself. Scoped impact: permanent DoS on all future signature-based agent authorizations for take, repay, withdraw, liquidate. Proof idea: victim authorizes EcrecoverAuthorizer and attacker on Midnight; attacker calls setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim); assert subsequent victim-signed auth via EcrecoverAuthorizer reverts with Unauthorized on Midnight.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=liquidatorAgent, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize a liquidator agent, under the precondition that victim is a borrower with an unhealthy position and the liquidator agent was authorized to call Midnight.liquidate on victim's behalf, thereby preventing liquidation and allowing bad debt to accumulate? Invariant tested: only unhealthy accounts should be liquidatable and liquidation must not be blockable by low-cost attacker actions. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidate via deauthorization of liquidator agent. Proof idea: set up unhealthy borrower with authorized liquidator agent; attacker (authorized by victim) calls setIsAuthorized to deauthorize liquidator; assert liquidate reverts with TakerUnauthorized or Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent repeatedly call setIsAuthorized with trivial no-op authorizations (e.g., Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=self, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N)) in a tight loop to exhaust nonce[victim] and invalidate a batch of pre-signed authorizations victim distributed to multiple agents, under the precondition that the attacker is authorized by victim on Midnight and gas is cheap, causing a sustained low-cost DoS? Invariant tested: nonce increment must require authorizer consent. Scoped impact: sustained DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, claimFee. Proof idea: fuzz loop of attacker-signed setIsAuthorized calls; assert each increments nonce[victim] and victim's pre-signed auths with old nonces all revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker with a valid signature for Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=agent, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=T) exploit Ethereum signature malleability (replacing s with secp256k1_order - s and flipping v) to produce a second valid signature for the same authorization struct, submit it after the original is consumed, and observe whether the nonce check or ecrecover behavior creates any inconsistency that could DoS the victim? Invariant tested: each signed authorization must be processable exactly once. Scoped impact: potential double-processing or nonce desync DoS. Proof idea: take a valid (v,r,s), compute (v^1, r, n-s mod order), submit both; assert second reverts with InvalidNonce and no state corruption.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker craft an Authorization with authorization.authorizer == address(0) and a signature that causes ecrecover to return address(0), satisfying the check signer == authorization.authorizer (both zero), under the precondition that ecrecover returns address(0) for degenerate inputs, to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized on behalf of address(0) and potentially corrupt authorization state? Invariant tested: signer != address(0) check must be sufficient to prevent zero-address authorizer bypass. Scoped impact: authorization state corruption or DoS via unexpected state changes. Proof idea: craft (v=0, r=0, s=0) signature with authorizer=address(0); assert setIsAuthorized reverts with InvalidSignature before reaching Midnight call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can a block proposer (or attacker bribing one) delay inclusion of a victim's setIsAuthorized transaction past authorization.deadline, under the precondition that the victim signed an Authorization with a tight deadline (e.g., block.timestamp + 1 block) to authorize an agent for an urgent repay or liquidation, causing the transaction to revert with Expired and blocking the time-sensitive action? Invariant tested: deadline-bounded authorizations must be processable within their validity window without miner interference. Scoped impact: DoS on time-sensitive repay or liquidate via deadline expiry. Proof idea: sign auth with deadline=block.timestamp+12s; warp time by 13s; assert setIsAuthorized reverts with Expired.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker observe that EcrecoverAuthorizer is not yet authorized on Midnight by a victim (isAuthorized[victim][EcrecoverAuthorizer]==false), and submit a valid victim-signed authorization through EcrecoverAuthorizer, causing the inner Midnight.setIsAuthorized call to revert with Unauthorized, under the precondition that the victim forgot to pre-authorize EcrecoverAuthorizer on Midnight, creating a bootstrapping DoS? Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must be authorized on Midnight before it can act on behalf of any authorizer. Scoped impact: DoS on all EcrecoverAuthorizer-based agent authorizations. Proof idea: deploy fresh EcrecoverAuthorizer; victim signs valid auth; submit without pre-authorizing EcrecoverAuthorizer on Midnight; assert revert with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=repayAgent, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize a repay agent just before a debt maturity deadline, under the precondition that victim is a borrower relying on an authorized agent to call Midnight.repay, causing the repay to fail and the position to become liquidatable post-maturity? Invariant tested: authorized repay agents must not be deauthorizable by other authorized agents without the authorizer's consent. Scoped impact: DoS on repay leading to forced post-maturity liquidation. Proof idea: victim authorizes repayAgent and attacker; attacker deauthorizes repayAgent via EcrecoverAuthorizer; warp to maturity; assert repayAgent's repay call reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=withdrawAgent, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize a withdraw agent, under the precondition that victim is a lender with credit in a market and relies on an authorized agent to call Midnight.withdraw, causing the lender's funds to be frozen? Invariant tested: lender withdrawal must not be blockable by a co-authorized agent. Scoped impact: DoS on withdraw, freezing lender funds. Proof idea: victim authorizes withdrawAgent and attacker; attacker deauthorizes withdrawAgent; assert withdrawAgent's withdraw call reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=feeClaimer, authorized=claimAgent, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize the fee claimer's agent, under the precondition that the feeClaimer relies on an authorized agent to call Midnight.claimSettlementFee or claimContinuousFee, causing fee claims to fail? Invariant tested: fee claim authorization must not be revocable by co-authorized agents. Scoped impact: DoS on claimSettlementFee and claimContinuousFee. Proof idea: feeClaimer authorizes claimAgent and attacker; attacker deauthorizes claimAgent via EcrecoverAuthorizer; assert claimAgent's claimSettlementFee call reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=takeAgent, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize a take agent, under the precondition that victim is a taker relying on an authorized agent to call Midnight.take for a time-sensitive offer (near expiry), causing the take to fail and the offer to expire? Invariant tested: take agent authorization must not be revocable by co-authorized agents. Scoped impact: DoS on take via deauthorization of take agent. Proof idea: victim authorizes takeAgent and attacker; attacker deauthorizes takeAgent; assert takeAgent's take call reverts with TakerUnauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized agent of victim call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) repeatedly (each call is a valid no-op re-authorization of self) to increment nonce[victim] by 1 per call at minimal gas cost, under the precondition that attacker is authorized on Midnight, to invalidate a batch of N pre-signed authorizations victim distributed offline? Invariant tested: nonce increment cost must be prohibitive for attackers. Scoped impact: sustained low-cost DoS on all pending signed authorizations for take, repay, withdraw, liquidate. Proof idea: fuzz N calls from attacker; assert nonce[victim] == N and all victim's pre-signed auths with nonce < N revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically (1) call setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves on Midnight via a victim's pre-signed auth, (2) call setIsAuthorized again via EcrecoverAuthorizer to deauthorize a critical agent, all in one transaction, under the precondition that victim has signed an authorization for attacker and EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized on Midnight? Invariant tested: multicall must not enable atomic authorization manipulation that single calls cannot. Scoped impact: atomic DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate via multicall-based authorization manipulation. Proof idea: craft multicall bundle; assert attacker gains authorization and critical agent loses it atomically.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit a setIsAuthorized call with a valid signature but with authorization.deadline == block.timestamp (boundary condition), under the precondition that the block timestamp equals the deadline exactly, to test whether the check block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline passes or fails at the boundary, potentially causing unexpected DoS if off-by-one? Invariant tested: deadline check must be inclusive at the boundary (<=). Scoped impact: DoS on authorization at exact deadline boundary. Proof idea: sign auth with deadline=block.timestamp; submit in same block; assert it succeeds (not reverts with Expired).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized agent of victim call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to set isAuthorized[victim][victim]=false on Midnight, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by victim, potentially causing unexpected behavior if any protocol path relies on self-authorization? Invariant tested: self-authorization state must not be manipulable by co-authorized agents. Scoped impact: potential DoS on any protocol path relying on isAuthorized[victim][victim]. Proof idea: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(victim, false, victim) via EcrecoverAuthorizer; assert isAuthorized[victim][victim]==false and check all protocol paths.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a victim's authorization of a liquidator agent (needed to liquidate an unhealthy borrower) by submitting the same signed authorization first, consuming the nonce, under the precondition that the borrower's position is unhealthy and approaching bad debt, causing the liquidation window to close and bad debt to be realized? Invariant tested: liquidation of unhealthy positions must not be blockable by front-running authorization transactions. Scoped impact: DoS on liquidate via nonce front-run, leading to bad debt accumulation. Proof idea: set up unhealthy borrower; victim signs auth for liquidator; attacker front-runs; assert liquidator cannot be authorized and liquidate reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that the EIP-712 domain separator in EcrecoverAuthorizer uses block.chainid (not INITIAL_CHAIN_ID as Midnight does) to replay a signed authorization from a chain where block.chainid matches INITIAL_CHAIN_ID on a forked chain where they differ, under the precondition that a hard fork has occurred, to authorize unintended agents and DoS legitimate operations? Invariant tested: domain separator must prevent cross-chain signature replay. Scoped impact: DoS via unauthorized agent authorization on forked chain. Proof idea: simulate chain fork; replay old-chain signature on new chain; assert ecrecover produces different signer due to domain separator change.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized agent call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=MidnightBundlesContract, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize the MidnightBundles contract from acting on victim's behalf, under the precondition that victim relies on MidnightBundles for bundled take/repay/withdrawCollateral operations, causing all bundled operations to fail with Unauthorized? Invariant tested: MidnightBundles authorization must not be revocable by co-authorized agents. Scoped impact: DoS on all MidnightBundles-based take, repay, withdrawCollateral operations. Proof idea: victim authorizes MidnightBundles and attacker; attacker deauthorizes MidnightBundles; assert buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit a setIsAuthorized call with a signature where v is not in {27, 28} (e.g., v=0 or v=1), causing ecrecover to return address(0), under the precondition that authorization.authorizer != address(0), to test whether the InvalidSignature check correctly rejects such inputs and does not accidentally pass? Invariant tested: ecrecover with invalid v must always return address(0) and be rejected. Scoped impact: potential signature bypass DoS if check is insufficient. Proof idea: fuzz v over all uint8 values; assert setIsAuthorized reverts with InvalidSignature for all v not in {27,28}.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized agent of victim call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker2, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N) to authorize a second attacker on Midnight on victim's behalf, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by victim, creating a chain of unauthorized agents that can further manipulate victim's authorizations and DoS their operations? Invariant tested: authorized agents must not be able to grant authorization to arbitrary third parties. Scoped impact: authorization chain escalation leading to DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate. Proof idea: attacker1 (authorized by victim) calls setIsAuthorized to authorize attacker2; assert isAuthorized[victim][attacker2]==true and attacker2 can now call setIsAuthorized on victim's behalf.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit a setIsAuthorized call with authorization.nonce == type(uint256).max, under the precondition that nonce[authorizer] has been incremented to type(uint256).max - 1 (via repeated griefing), causing nonce[authorizer]++ to overflow to 0 in Solidity 0.8.x (which would revert with overflow), creating a permanent DoS on all future authorizations? Invariant tested: nonce overflow must not permanently lock the authorizer. Scoped impact: permanent DoS on all future EcrecoverAuthorizer-based authorizations. Proof idea: increment nonce to type(uint256).max; attempt setIsAuthorized; assert overflow revert behavior in Solidity 0.8.34.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized agent of victim call setIsAuthorized with Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=ratifierContract, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to deauthorize a ratifier contract that victim's maker offers depend on (since take checks isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier]), under the precondition that victim is a maker with active offers using a ratifier, causing all victim's offers to fail the RatifierUnauthorized check? Invariant tested: ratifier authorization must not be revocable by co-authorized agents. Scoped impact: DoS on take for all of victim's maker offers via ratifier deauthorization. Proof idea: victim authorizes ratifier and attacker; attacker deauthorizes ratifier; assert take reverts with RatifierUnauthorized for victim's offers.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit multiple setIsAuthorized calls in the same block (via multicall or separate transactions) each with a different authorized address but same authorizer, under the precondition that attacker is authorized by victim, to atomically deauthorize all of victim's agents in a single block, creating a complete DoS on all victim's protocol interactions? Invariant tested: bulk deauthorization must not be achievable in a single block by an attacker. Scoped impact: complete DoS on take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, claimFee for victim. Proof idea: attacker submits N deauthorization calls in one block; assert all victim's agents are deauthorized simultaneously.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run a victim's pending setIsAuthorized transaction in the mempool under the precondition that the victim has broadcast a signed Authorization with nonce==nonce[victim] to authorize an agent for take/repay/withdraw, by submitting the identical (authorization, signature) tuple first, consuming nonce[victim] and incrementing it, so the victim's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce, blocking the agent authorization indefinitely at the cost of a single transaction?
